---
title: Quickstart
description: Build a Calimero app to WASM, install it on a node, attach it to a context, and call a method.
sidebar:
  order: 2
  badge:
    text: Draft
    variant: caution
---

import { Steps, Aside, Tabs, TabItem } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

This walks from an empty crate to a method call. The two binaries you use are
**`merod`** (the node daemon â€” `init`, `run`) and **`meroctl`** (the operator
CLI â€” `app`, `context`, `namespace`, `call`).

<Aside type="caution">
  Commands are grounded in the in-repo CLI definitions (`crates/merod/src/cli/`,
  `crates/meroctl/src/cli/`) and the example apps' `build.sh` scripts. The exact
  context-bootstrap surface (namespaces / groups) is evolving â€” where a step is
  not fully verifiable from a single source it is flagged inline. Run
  `meroctl <command> --help` to confirm flags for your build.
</Aside>

## 1. Write and build the app

<Steps>

1. Create a library crate with a WASM-compatible `crate-type`. A minimal
   `Cargo.toml`, modelled on `apps/kv-store/Cargo.toml`:

   ```toml
   [lib]
   crate-type = ["cdylib", "rlib"]

   [dependencies]
   calimero-sdk = { git = "https://github.com/calimero-network/core" }
   calimero-storage = { git = "https://github.com/calimero-network/core" }

   [dev-dependencies]
   # native test harness (TestHost) + CRDT merge under `cargo test`
   calimero-storage = { git = "https://github.com/calimero-network/core", features = ["testing"] }
   ```

   :::caution
   The dependency lines above show the crate names; pin them to a tag, rev, or
   workspace path as appropriate for your setup. The in-repo example apps use
   `calimero-sdk.workspace = true`.
   :::

2. Write the smallest possible app â€” a state struct plus one method. This is the
   shape every Calimero app follows (see [SDK macros](/build/sdk-macros/)):

   ```rust
   use calimero_sdk::app;
   use calimero_storage::collections::{LwwRegister, UnorderedMap};

   #[app::state]
   pub struct KvStore {
       items: UnorderedMap<String, LwwRegister<String>>,
   }

   #[app::logic]
   impl KvStore {
       #[app::init]
       pub fn init() -> KvStore {
           KvStore { items: UnorderedMap::new() }
       }

       pub fn set(&mut self, key: String, value: String) -> app::Result<()> {
           self.items.insert(key, value.into())?;
           Ok(())
       }

       #[app::view]
       pub fn get(&self, key: String) -> app::Result<Option<String>> {
           Ok(self.items.get(&key)?.map(|v| v.get().clone()))
       }
   }
   ```

3. Build to WASM. The example apps ship a `build.sh`; the load-bearing line is:

   ```sh
   rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown
   cargo build --target wasm32-unknown-unknown --profile app-release
   ```

   The artifact lands at
   `target/wasm32-unknown-unknown/app-release/<crate_name>.wasm`. The example
   scripts then copy it into a local `res/` directory and (if `wasm-opt` is
   installed) shrink it:

   ```sh
   wasm-opt -Oz --enable-bulk-memory ./res/my_app.wasm -o ./res/my_app.wasm
   ```

   :::note
   The `app-release` (and `app-profiling`) profiles are defined in the workspace.
   Building a standalone crate outside the workspace? Either copy those profiles
   into your `Cargo.toml` or use `--release` instead.
   :::

</Steps>

## 2. Run a node

<Steps>

1. Initialize a node's config and data directory, then run it:

   ```sh
   merod --node node1 init --server-port 2428 --swarm-port 2528
   merod --node node1 run
   ```

   `merod --node node1 init --help` lists the full set (swarm/server host and
   port, boot nodes, mDNS, auth mode). Config and data live under
   `CALIMERO_HOME` / `--home`.

</Steps>

## 3. Install the app, attach a context, call a method

`meroctl` talks to a running node. Select the node with `--node node1` (a
configured alias) or `--api <URL>`.

<Steps>

1. **Install** the WASM module. The node returns an application id:

   ```sh
   meroctl --node node1 app install \
     --path ./res/my_app.wasm \
     --package com.example.myapp \
     --version 1.0.0
   ```

   List what is installed with `meroctl --node node1 app ls`.

2. **Attach the app to a context.** A context is a running instance of an app
   over a shared state. The simplest entry is to create a namespace bound to the
   application id:

   ```sh
   meroctl --node node1 namespace create --application-id <app-id>
   ```

   :::caution
   `meroctl context create` also exists and takes `--application-id`, but in the
   current CLI it additionally requires a `--group-id` (and optionally `--as`,
   `--name`, `--params`). The namespace/group bootstrap is the part most in
   flux â€” confirm the exact flow with `meroctl namespace --help` and
   `meroctl context create --help` before scripting it.
   :::

3. **Call a method** on the context. Mutations and views both go through the
   top-level `call` command:

   ```sh
   # mutate
   meroctl --node node1 call set \
     --context <context-id> \
     --args '{"key": "hello", "value": "world"}'

   # view (optionally as a specific identity)
   meroctl --node node1 call get \
     --context <context-id> \
     --args '{"key": "hello"}' \
     --as <identity-public-key>
   ```

   `--args` takes JSON matching the method's parameters. `<context-id>` accepts a
   context id or an alias you created with `--name`.

</Steps>

<Aside type="tip" title="Test before you ship">
  The SDK ships a native in-process harness (`calimero_sdk::testing::TestHost`,
  enabled by the `calimero-storage` `testing` feature). You can drive your app's
  methods and assert on state under plain `cargo test`, with no node and no WASM
  build. See the `tests/` directories of the example apps.
</Aside>
